subject: Astronomy & Space
title: The Radial Velocity Method
description: A 60-second lesson on The Radial Velocity Method.

hook.text: A planet's gravity tugs on its star, making the star wobble. That wobble shifts the star's light in a detectable way.

buildup.text: As the star moves toward us, its light shifts blue. As it moves away, it shifts red. This is the Doppler effect.

discovery.text: Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz used this method to find 51 Pegasi b in 1995—earning them a Nobel Prize in 2019.

twist.text: This method reveals a planet's mass and orbit but not its size. It's complementary to the transit method.

climax.text: Modern spectrographs can detect star wobbles of just 1 m/s—a slow walking speed from light-years away.

punchline.text: Stars wobble when planets pull. That wobble crosses the galaxy to reach our instruments.

quiz.question: What does the radial velocity method measure to find exoplanets?
options: The wobble of a star caused by a planet's gravitational pull
The speed at which a planet orbits its star
Changes in a star's brightness over time
